verses.original_text: σκηνὴ γὰρ κατεσκευάσθη ἡ πρώτη ἐν ᾗ ἥ τε λυχνία καὶ ἡ τράπεζα καὶ ἡ πρόθεσις τῶν ἄρτων ἥτις λέγεται ἅγια
verses.transliteration: skēnē gar kateskeuasthē hē prōtē en hē hē te lychnia kai hē trapeza kai hē prothesis tōn artōn hētis legetai hagia
verses.study_note: 'The first' (hē prōtē) — the first compartment, the Holy Place (hagia). Three items: (1) 'the candlestick' (hē lychnia — the golden lampstand with seven branches, Exod 25:31-40); (2) 'the table' (hē trapeza — the table of showbread, Exod 25:23-30); (3) 'the shewbread' (hē prothesis tōn artōn — the bread of the Presence, twelve loaves representing the twelve tribes, renewed weekly). Each item had symbolic significance: the lampstand (God's light/presence), the table with bread (God's provision/fellowship).

verses.kjv_text: And after the second veil, the tabernacle which is called the Holiest of all;
verses.original_text: μετὰ δὲ τὸ δεύτερον καταπέτασμα σκηνὴ ἡ λεγομένη ἅγια ἁγίων
verses.transliteration: meta de to deuteron katapetasma skēnē hē legomenē hagia hagiōn
verses.study_note: 'The second veil' (to deuteron katapetasma — the inner curtain separating the Holy Place from the Holy of Holies). 'The Holiest of all' (hagia hagiōn — the Holy of Holies, literally 'holies of holies,' the superlative expression). This innermost chamber was the earthly dwelling place of God's presence — entered only once a year by the high priest on the Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur). The veil symbolized the separation between God and sinful humanity — a separation that Christ's death would definitively remove (Matt 27:51).

verses.kjv_text: Which had the golden censer, and the ark of the covenant overlaid round about with gold, wherein was the golden pot that had manna, and Aaron's rod that budded, and the tables of the covenant;
verses.original_text: χρυσοῦν ἔχουσα θυμιατήριον καὶ τὴν κιβωτὸν τῆς διαθήκης περικεκαλυμμένην πάντοθεν χρυσίῳ ἐν ᾗ στάμνος χρυσῆ ἔχουσα τὸ μάννα καὶ ἡ ῥάβδος Ἀαρὼν ἡ βλαστήσασα καὶ αἱ πλάκες τῆς διαθήκης
verses.transliteration: chrysoun echousa thymatērion kai tēn kibōton tēs diathēkēs perikekalymmenēn pantothen chrysiō en hē stamnos chrysē echousa to manna kai hē rhabdos Aarōn hē blastēsasa kai hai plakes tēs diathēkēs
verses.study_note: 'The golden censer' (chrysoun thymatērion — a golden incense altar or censer; likely the altar of incense that stood before the veil but was functionally associated with the Holy of Holies). 'The ark of the covenant' (tēn kibōton tēs diathēkēs) — the most sacred object in Israel, containing three items: (1) 'the golden pot that had manna' — memorial of God's wilderness provision (Exod 16:33); (2) 'Aaron's rod that budded' — proof of God's chosen priesthood (Num 17:10); (3) 'the tables of the covenant' — the Ten Commandments, the terms of the Sinai covenant (Exod 25:16).

verses.kjv_text: And over it the cherubims of glory shadowing the mercyseat; of which we cannot now speak particularly.
verses.original_text: ὑπεράνω δὲ αὐτῆς χερουβὶμ δόξης κατασκιάζοντα τὸ ἱλαστήριον περὶ ὧν οὐκ ἔστιν νῦν λέγειν κατὰ μέρος
verses.transliteration: hyperanō de autēs cheroubim doxēs kataskiazonta to hilastērion peri hōn ouk estin nyn legein kata meros
verses.study_note: 'Cherubims of glory' (cheroubim doxēs — the golden cherubim whose wings overshadowed the mercy seat, representing God's throne-guardians). 'Shadowing the mercyseat' (kataskiazonta to hilastērion). The 'mercyseat' (hilastērion — the place of propitiation, the atonement cover; from hilaskomai, to propitiate). This is where the high priest sprinkled blood on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:14-15) — where God's justice met God's mercy, where the blood covered sin. Paul uses the same word (hilastērion) in Romans 3:25 to describe Christ Himself as the propitiation. 'We cannot now speak particularly' — the author restrains himself; his focus is not the furniture but the function of the system.

verses.kjv_text: Now when these things were thus ordained, the priests went always into the first tabernacle, accomplishing the service of God.
verses.original_text: τούτων δὲ οὕτως κατεσκευασμένων εἰς μὲν τὴν πρώτην σκηνὴν διὰ παντὸς εἰσίασιν οἱ ἱερεῖς τὰς λατρείας ἐπιτελοῦντες
verses.transliteration: toutōn de houtōs kateskeuasmenōn eis men tēn prōtēn skēnēn dia pantos eisiasin hoi hiereis tas latreias epitelountes
verses.study_note: 'Always' (dia pantos — continually, at all times). The ordinary priests entered the Holy Place daily to tend the lampstand, offer incense, and replace the showbread. This daily access to the first compartment is contrasted with the restricted access to the second — highlighting the limited nature of the old system.

verses.kjv_text: But into the second went the high priest alone once every year, not without blood, which he offered for himself, and for the errors of the people:
verses.original_text: εἰς δὲ τὴν δευτέραν ἅπαξ τοῦ ἐνιαυτοῦ μόνος ὁ ἀρχιερεύς οὐ χωρὶς αἵματος ὃ προσφέρει ὑπὲρ ἑαυτοῦ καὶ τῶν τοῦ λαοῦ ἀγνοημάτων
verses.transliteration: eis de tēn deuteran hapax tou eniautou monos ho archiereus ou chōris haimatos ho prospherei hyper heautou kai tōn tou laou agnoēmatōn
verses.study_note: 'Into the second' — the Holy of Holies. 'Alone' (monos — only the high priest, no one else). 'Once every year' (hapax tou eniautou — once per year, on the Day of Atonement). 'Not without blood' (ou chōris haimatos — never without blood). Three restrictions: (1) only one person; (2) only one day per year; (3) always with blood. 'For himself, and for the errors of the people' (hyper heautou kai tōn tou laou agnoēmatōn — for his own sins and for the people's sins of ignorance). The high priest had to offer for himself first — his own sinfulness was a constant limitation.
verses.doctrinal_note: The restricted access to the Holy of Holies — one person, one day, with blood — powerfully symbolized the barrier between God and sinful humanity. The very restrictions testified that the way to God was 'not yet made manifest' (v.8). Christ removed all three restrictions: all believers (not just one priest) may approach God at any time (not just one day) through His blood (not animal blood).

verses.kjv_text: The Holy Ghost this signifying, that the way into the holiest of all was not yet made manifest, while as the first tabernacle was yet standing:
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δηλοῦντος τοῦ πνεύματος τοῦ ἁγίου μήπω πεφανερῶσθαι τὴν τῶν ἁγίων ὁδὸν ἔτι τῆς πρώτης σκηνῆς ἐχούσης στάσιν
verses.transliteration: touto dēlountos tou pneumatos tou hagiou mēpō pephanerōsthai tēn tōn hagiōn hodon eti tēs prōtēs skēnēs echousēs stasin
verses.study_note: 'The Holy Ghost this signifying' (touto dēlountos tou pneumatos tou hagiou — the Holy Spirit making this clear/evident). The Spirit Himself, through the tabernacle design, was communicating a theological truth: 'the way into the holiest of all was not yet made manifest' (mēpō pephanerōsthai — not yet revealed/opened). 'While the first tabernacle was yet standing' — as long as the old system functioned, it testified to its own inadequacy. The veil declared: 'You cannot come in.' The annual repetition declared: 'The problem is not yet solved.' The old system was a prophetic sign pointing to its own replacement.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is a remarkable hermeneutical statement: the Holy Spirit designed the tabernacle structure itself as a theological teaching tool. The very architecture — the veil barring access, the annual-only entrance — was the Spirit's way of saying: 'The way to God is not yet open; a better way is coming.' The old covenant's limitations were intentional, pedagogical, and temporary.

verses.kjv_text: Which was a figure for the time then present, in which were offered both gifts and sacrifices, that could not make him that did the service perfect, as pertaining to the conscience:
verses.original_text: ἥτις παραβολὴ εἰς τὸν καιρὸν τὸν ἐνεστηκότα καθ᾽ ὃν δῶρά τε καὶ θυσίαι προσφέρονται μὴ δυνάμεναι κατὰ συνείδησιν τελειῶσαι τὸν λατρεύοντα
verses.transliteration: hētis parabolē eis ton kairon ton enestēkota kath hon dōra te kai thysiai prospherontai mē dynamenai kata syneidēsin teleiōsai ton latreuonta
verses.study_note: 'A figure' (parabolē — a parable, an illustration, a symbolic representation). 'Could not make him … perfect, as pertaining to the conscience' (mē dynamenai kata syneidēsin teleiōsai — unable to bring to completion regarding the conscience). This is the old system's fatal deficiency: it could address external/ceremonial uncleanness but could not reach the conscience — the inner awareness of guilt before God. Animal blood could ritually purify the body but could not remove the consciousness of sin. The worshipper walked away from the sacrifice still burdened with inner guilt.
verses.doctrinal_note: The old covenant's inability to purify the conscience is the crucial limitation that Christ's sacrifice addresses. Conscience (syneidēsis) is the inner awareness of moral standing before God. Animal sacrifices could not reach this deep — they dealt with the surface, not the root. Christ's blood purges the conscience itself (v.14), achieving what the entire Levitical system could not.

verses.kjv_text: Which stood only in meats and drinks, and divers washings, and carnal ordinances, imposed on them until the time of reformation.
verses.original_text: μόνον ἐπὶ βρώμασιν καὶ πόμασιν καὶ διαφόροις βαπτισμοῖς καὶ δικαιώμασιν σαρκὸς μέχρι καιροῦ διορθώσεως ἐπικείμενα
verses.transliteration: monon epi brōmasin kai pomasin kai diaphorois baptismois kai dikaiōmasin sarkos mechri kairou diorthōseōs epikeimena
verses.study_note: 'Meats and drinks' (brōmasin kai pomasin — food and drink regulations). 'Divers washings' (diaphorois baptismois — various ceremonial washings). 'Carnal ordinances' (dikaiōmasin sarkos — regulations pertaining to the flesh/body). 'Imposed until the time of reformation' (mechri kairou diorthōseōs — until the appointed time of setting things right). Diorthōsis means correction, reformation, setting straight — the time when God would replace the external system with an internal reality. The old covenant regulated the external (what you eat, how you wash, bodily practices) but could not transform the internal. Christ's coming is the 'reformation' — the time when the shadows give way to the substance.

verses.kjv_text: But Christ being come an high priest of good things to come, by a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands, that is to say, not of this building;
verses.original_text: Χριστὸς δὲ παραγενόμενος ἀρχιερεὺς τῶν μελλόντων ἀγαθῶν διὰ τῆς μείζονος καὶ τελειοτέρας σκηνῆς οὐ χειροποιήτου τοῦτ᾽ ἔστιν οὐ ταύτης τῆς κτίσεως
verses.transliteration: Christos de paragenomenos archiereus tōn mellontōn agathōn dia tēs meizonos kai teleioteras skēnēs ou cheiropoiētou tout estin ou tautēs tēs ktiseōs
verses.study_note: 'But Christ' (Christos de — the great adversative: everything changes). 'High priest of good things to come' (archiereus tōn mellontōn agathōn — high priest of the coming good things, the eschatological blessings). 'A greater and more perfect tabernacle' (meizonos kai teleioteras skēnēs — a larger and more complete tent/sanctuary). 'Not made with hands' (ou cheiropoiētou — not manufactured by human effort). 'Not of this building/creation' (ou tautēs tēs ktiseōs — not belonging to this created order). Christ ministers in a sanctuary that belongs to a different order of reality — the heavenly, the eternal, the uncreated.

verses.kjv_text: Neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood he entered in once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption for us.
verses.original_text: οὐδὲ δι᾽ αἵματος τράγων καὶ μόσχων διὰ δὲ τοῦ ἰδίου αἵματος εἰσῆλθεν ἐφάπαξ εἰς τὰ ἅγια αἰωνίαν λύτρωσιν εὑράμενος
verses.transliteration: oude di haimatos tragōn kai moschōn dia de tou idiou haimatos eisēlthen ephapax eis ta hagia aiōnian lytrōsin heuramenos
verses.study_note: 'Not by the blood of goats and calves' — the contrast with the Day of Atonement (Lev 16). 'By his own blood' (dia tou idiou haimatos — through His own blood). 'Once' (ephapax — once for all, a single unrepeatable act; the same word as 7:27). 'Into the holy place' (eis ta hagia — into the holy places, the heavenly sanctuary). 'Having obtained eternal redemption' (aiōnian lytrōsin heuramenos — having found/secured everlasting redemption). 'Redemption' (lytrōsis — release effected by payment of a ransom price; from lytron, a ransom). The price: His own blood. The result: eternal (not annual) redemption. The act: once (not repeated).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the sacrificial center of Hebrews. Three contrasts define Christ's superior offering: (1) not animal blood but His own blood; (2) not repeated annually but once for all; (3) not temporary ceremonial cleansing but eternal redemption. Every deficiency of the old system is answered in a single verse.

verses.kjv_text: For if the blood of bulls and of goats, and the ashes of an heifer sprinkling the unclean, sanctifieth to the purifying of the flesh:
verses.original_text: εἰ γὰρ τὸ αἷμα ταύρων καὶ τράγων καὶ σποδὸς δαμάλεως ῥαντίζουσα τοὺς κεκοινωμένους ἁγιάζει πρὸς τὴν τῆς σαρκὸς καθαρότητα
verses.transliteration: ei gar to haima taurōn kai tragōn kai spodos damaleōs rantizousa tous kekoinōmenous hagiazei pros tēn tēs sarkos katharotēta
verses.study_note: 'Blood of bulls and goats' — the Day of Atonement sacrifices. 'Ashes of an heifer' (spodos damaleōs) — the red heifer ceremony (Num 19), whose ashes mixed with water purified those who had contacted a corpse. 'Sanctifieth to the purifying of the flesh' (hagiazei pros tēn tēs sarkos katharotēta — sanctifies for bodily/ceremonial cleanness). The argument is from the lesser to the greater (a fortiori): if animal blood achieves external purification (which it demonstrably does), how much more …

verses.kjv_text: How much more shall the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God, purge your conscience from dead works to serve the living God?
verses.original_text: πόσῳ μᾶλλον τὸ αἷμα τοῦ Χριστοῦ ὃς διὰ πνεύματος αἰωνίου ἑαυτὸν προσήνεγκεν ἄμωμον τῷ θεῷ καθαριεῖ τὴν συνείδησιν ὑμῶν ἀπὸ νεκρῶν ἔργων εἰς τὸ λατρεύειν θεῷ ζῶντι
verses.transliteration: posō mallon to haima tou Christou hos dia pneumatos aiōniou heauton prosēnenken amōmon tō theō kathariei tēn syneidēsin hymōn apo nekrōn ergōn eis to latreuein theō zōnti
verses.study_note: 'How much more' (posō mallon — the a fortiori argument reaches its climax). 'The blood of Christ' — His life given in death. 'Through the eternal Spirit' (dia pneumatos aiōniou — through the Holy Spirit, the eternal Spirit). Christ's self-offering was Spirit-empowered — the Trinity acted together in the atonement: the Father sent, the Son offered, the Spirit empowered. 'Without spot' (amōmon — unblemished, without defect; the sacrificial term for an animal without blemish). 'Purge your conscience' (kathariei tēn syneidēsin — will cleanse the conscience). 'From dead works' (apo nekrōn ergōn — from works that produce death, works done apart from faith). 'To serve the living God' (eis to latreuein theō zōnti — for the purpose of worshipping the living God). The cleansed conscience enables genuine worship.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the soteriological center of Hebrews. The blood of Christ does what animal blood never could: it purges the conscience — the innermost seat of moral awareness. The trinitarian dimension is explicit: Christ offered Himself through the eternal Spirit to God. And the purpose of cleansing is not merely forgiveness but worship — freed from the burden of guilty conscience, believers can now serve the living God with joy and freedom.

verses.kjv_text: And for this cause he is the mediator of the new testament, that by means of death, for the redemption of the transgressions that were under the first testament, they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance.
verses.original_text: καὶ διὰ τοῦτο διαθήκης καινῆς μεσίτης ἐστίν ὅπως θανάτου γενομένου εἰς ἀπολύτρωσιν τῶν ἐπὶ τῇ πρώτῃ διαθήκῃ παραβάσεων τὴν ἐπαγγελίαν λάβωσιν οἱ κεκλημένοι τῆς αἰωνίου κληρονομίας
verses.transliteration: kai dia touto diathēkēs kainēs mesitēs estin hopōs thanatou genomenou eis apolytrōsin tōn epi tē prōtē diathēkē parabaseōn tēn epangelian labōsin hoi keklēmenoi tēs aiōniou klēronomias
verses.study_note: 'Mediator of the new testament' (diathēkēs kainēs mesitēs). 'By means of death' (thanatou genomenou — a death having occurred). 'For the redemption of the transgressions that were under the first testament' — Christ's death is retroactive: it redeems transgressions committed under the old covenant. The OT saints were saved by faith looking forward to what Christ would accomplish. 'They which are called' (hoi keklēmenoi — the called ones, those divinely summoned). 'Eternal inheritance' (tēs aiōniou klēronomias) — the ultimate inheritance that the earthly promised land only foreshadowed.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christ's death has retroactive efficacy: it redeems transgressions committed under the first covenant. The OT sacrifices never actually removed sin — they pointed forward to the sacrifice that would. Christ's blood is the reality to which all OT sacrifices pointed; His death is the ground on which every OT believer was saved.

verses.kjv_text: For where a testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of the testator.
verses.original_text: ὅπου γὰρ διαθήκη θάνατον ἀνάγκη φέρεσθαι τοῦ διαθεμένου
verses.transliteration: hopou gar diathēkē thanaton anankē pheresthai tou diathemenou
verses.study_note: 'Where a testament is' (hopou diathēkē — where a will/testament exists). The author exploits the double meaning of diathēkē: both 'covenant' and 'testament/will.' 'There must be the death of the testator' (thanaton anankē pheresthai tou diathemenou — the death of the one who made the will must be established). A will only takes effect when the testator dies. Christ is both the testator (who made the testament) and the sacrifice (who died to enact it). His death activated the new covenant — the 'inheritance' (v.15) becomes available through the testator's death.

verses.kjv_text: For a testament is of force after men are dead: otherwise it is of no strength at all while the testator liveth.
verses.original_text: διαθήκη γὰρ ἐπὶ νεκροῖς βεβαία ἐπεὶ μή ποτε ἰσχύει ὅτε ζῇ ὁ διαθέμενος
verses.transliteration: diathēkē gar epi nekrois bebaia epei mē pote ischyei hote zē ho diathemenos
verses.study_note: 'Of force after men are dead' (epi nekrois bebaia — confirmed/ratified upon the dead, i.e., when death has occurred). 'No strength at all while the testator liveth' — a will has no legal force while the testator is alive; it can be changed, revoked, or annulled. Only death makes it irrevocable. So Christ's death made the new covenant irrevocable — it cannot be altered, recalled, or superseded. The covenant is sealed in blood — permanent, unchangeable, final.

verses.kjv_text: Whereupon neither the first testament was dedicated without blood.
verses.original_text: ὅθεν οὐδ᾽ ἡ πρώτη χωρὶς αἵματος ἐγκεκαίνισται
verses.transliteration: hothen oud hē prōtē chōris haimatos enkekainistai
verses.study_note: 'Dedicated' (enkekainistai — inaugurated, ratified, put into effect; from enkainizō, to renew, to inaugurate). Even the first covenant required blood for its inauguration (Exod 24:5-8). The principle is consistent throughout Scripture: covenants are ratified by blood — life given in death. The shedding of blood represents the seriousness of the covenant commitment and the cost of its violation.

verses.kjv_text: For when Moses had spoken every precept to all the people according to the law, he took the blood of calves and of goats, with water, and scarlet wool, and hyssop, and sprinkled both the book, and all the people,
verses.original_text: λαληθείσης γὰρ πάσης ἐντολῆς κατὰ νόμον ὑπὸ Μωϋσέως παντὶ τῷ λαῷ λαβὼν τὸ αἷμα τῶν μόσχων καὶ τράγων μετὰ ὕδατος καὶ ἐρίου κοκκίνου καὶ ὑσσώπου αὐτό τε τὸ βιβλίον καὶ πάντα τὸν λαὸν ἐρράντισεν
verses.transliteration: lalētheisēs gar pasēs entolēs kata nomon hypo Mōyseōs panti tō laō labōn to haima tōn moschōn kai tragōn meta hydatos kai eriou kokkinou kai hyssōpou auto te to biblion kai panta ton laon errantisen
verses.study_note: Recounting the Sinai covenant inauguration (Exod 24:3-8). Moses sprinkled the blood on both the book (the covenant document) and the people (the covenant community). Blood touched everything — binding both parties, sanctifying the agreement, and making it solemn and irrevocable. 'Scarlet wool and hyssop' — instruments of application, used in purification rituals (Lev 14:4-6; Num 19:6).

verses.kjv_text: Saying, This is the blood of the testament which God hath enjoined unto you.
verses.original_text: λέγων τοῦτο τὸ αἷμα τῆς διαθήκης ἧς ἐνετείλατο πρὸς ὑμᾶς ὁ θεός
verses.transliteration: legōn touto to haima tēs diathēkēs hēs eneteilato pros hymas ho theos
verses.study_note: 'This is the blood of the testament' — echoing Exodus 24:8 and anticipating Jesus' words at the Last Supper: 'This is my blood of the new testament' (Matt 26:28). The verbal parallel is deliberate and profound: Jesus consciously identified His death with the covenant-inaugurating sacrifice. What Moses did with animal blood at Sinai, Christ did with His own blood at Calvary — but with infinitely greater effect.

verses.kjv_text: Moreover he sprinkled with blood both the tabernacle, and all the vessels of the ministry.
verses.original_text: καὶ τὴν σκηνὴν δὲ καὶ πάντα τὰ σκεύη τῆς λειτουργίας τῷ αἵματι ὁμοίως ἐρράντισεν
verses.transliteration: kai tēn skēnēn de kai panta ta skeuē tēs leitourgias tō haimati homoiōs errantisen
verses.study_note: The tabernacle and all its furnishings were sprinkled with blood. Nothing in the worship system escaped the blood — every object used in divine service was consecrated through blood-sprinkling. This comprehensive blood-application establishes the principle of v.22: without blood, no purification is possible.

verses.kjv_text: And almost all things are by the law purged with blood; and without shedding of blood is no remission.
verses.original_text: καὶ σχεδὸν ἐν αἵματι πάντα καθαρίζεται κατὰ τὸν νόμον καὶ χωρὶς αἱματεκχυσίας οὐ γίνεται ἄφεσις
verses.transliteration: kai schedon en haimati panta katharizetai kata ton nomon kai chōris haimatekchysias ou ginetai aphesis
verses.study_note: 'Almost all things' (schedon panta — nearly everything; 'almost' because some purifications used water or fire, cf. Num 31:22-23; Lev 5:11-13). 'Purged with blood' (en haimati katharizetai). 'Without shedding of blood is no remission' (chōris haimatekchysias ou ginetai aphesis). Haimatekchysia (blood-shedding) is a NT hapax — a compound word meaning 'the pouring out of blood.' Aphesis means release, forgiveness, letting go. The axiom is absolute: no forgiveness without blood. This is grounded in Leviticus 17:11 — 'the life of the flesh is in the blood … it is the blood that maketh an atonement for the soul.' Blood represents life given in death as a substitute.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse states the fundamental principle of biblical atonement: 'without shedding of blood is no remission.' Forgiveness requires a life given — a substitute who bears the penalty. This is not arbitrary but reflects the gravity of sin against a holy God. The principle applies both to the old covenant (animal blood) and the new (Christ's blood), but only the latter achieves actual, permanent remission.

verses.kjv_text: It was therefore necessary that the patterns of things in the heavens should be purified with these; but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these.
verses.original_text: ἀνάγκη οὖν τὰ μὲν ὑποδείγματα τῶν ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς τούτοις καθαρίζεσθαι αὐτὰ δὲ τὰ ἐπουράνια κρείττοσιν θυσίαις παρὰ ταύτας
verses.transliteration: anankē oun ta men hypodeigmata tōn en tois ouranois toutois katharizesthai auta de ta epourania kreittosin thysiais para tautas
verses.study_note: 'The patterns' (ta hypodeigmata — the copies/models; the earthly sanctuary). 'Purified with these' — animal blood sufficed for purifying the copies. 'The heavenly things themselves' (auta ta epourania — the heavenly realities themselves). 'Better sacrifices' (kreittosin thysiais — superior offerings). The plural 'sacrifices' likely refers to the one sacrifice of Christ viewed in its multiple aspects (His blood, His body, His life). Even the heavenly sanctuary needed 'purification' — not from physical contamination but from the claims of sin against the people it serves.

verses.kjv_text: For Christ is not entered into the holy places made with hands, which are the figures of the true; but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God for us:
verses.original_text: οὐ γὰρ εἰς χειροποίητα ἅγια εἰσῆλθεν ὁ Χριστός ἀντίτυπα τῶν ἀληθινῶν ἀλλ᾽ εἰς αὐτὸν τὸν οὐρανόν νῦν ἐμφανισθῆναι τῷ προσώπῳ τοῦ θεοῦ ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν
verses.transliteration: ou gar eis cheiropoiēta hagia eisēlthen ho Christos antitypa tōn alēthinōn all eis auton ton ouranon nyn emphanisthēnai tō prosōpō tou theou hyper hēmōn
verses.study_note: 'Not … the holy places made with hands' (ou cheiropoiēta hagia — not the handmade sanctuaries). 'The figures of the true' (antitypa tōn alēthinōn — the counterparts/copies of the genuine realities). 'Into heaven itself' (eis auton ton ouranon — into heaven itself, the genuine, uncreated, divine dwelling). 'To appear in the presence of God' (emphanisthēnai tō prosōpō tou theou — to appear before the face of God). 'For us' (hyper hēmōn — on our behalf). Christ stands in God's presence as our representative — His presence there guarantees our access. He is not performing a ritual but presenting a person — Himself, bearing the marks of His completed sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: Nor yet that he should offer himself often, as the high priest entereth into the holy place every year with blood of others;
verses.original_text: οὐδ᾽ ἵνα πολλάκις προσφέρῃ ἑαυτόν ὥσπερ ὁ ἀρχιερεὺς εἰσέρχεται εἰς τὰ ἅγια κατ᾽ ἐνιαυτόν ἐν αἵματι ἀλλοτρίῳ
verses.transliteration: oud hina pollakis prospherē heauton hōsper ho archiereus eiserchtai eis ta hagia kat eniauton en haimati allotriō
verses.study_note: 'Not … offer himself often' (oud hina pollakis prospherē heauton — not to offer Himself repeatedly). 'Every year with blood of others' (kat eniauton en haimati allotriō — annually with alien/foreign blood, i.e., animal blood). Two contrasts: (1) not repeatedly but once; (2) not with another's blood but His own. Repetition implied insufficiency; Christ's singular offering implies completeness.

verses.kjv_text: For then must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world: but now once in the end of the world hath he appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself.
verses.original_text: ἐπεὶ ἔδει αὐτὸν πολλάκις παθεῖν ἀπὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου νῦν δὲ ἅπαξ ἐπὶ συντελείᾳ τῶν αἰώνων εἰς ἀθέτησιν ἁμαρτίας διὰ τῆς θυσίας αὐτοῦ πεφανέρωται
verses.transliteration: epei edei auton pollakis pathein apo katabolēs kosmou nyn de hapax epi synteleia tōn aiōnōn eis athetēsin hamartias dia tēs thysias autou pephanerōtai
verses.study_note: 'Once in the end of the world' (hapax epi synteleia tōn aiōnōn — once at the consummation of the ages). 'To put away sin' (eis athetēsin hamartias — for the annulment, the cancellation, the abolition of sin; the same athetēsis from 7:18, meaning to set aside permanently). 'By the sacrifice of himself' (dia tēs thysias autou — through His own sacrifice). Christ appeared at the climactic moment of all history — the convergence point of all ages — to accomplish in one act what endless repetitions could never achieve: the permanent removal of sin. The incarnation and crucifixion are not accidents of history but the planned climax of all God's redemptive purposes.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christ's appearance 'in the end of the world' (at the consummation of the ages) means His sacrifice is the climactic event of all history. Everything before pointed toward it; everything after flows from it. The cross is the hinge of history — the moment toward which all creation was moving and from which all redemption proceeds.

verses.kjv_text: And as it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment:
verses.original_text: καὶ καθ᾽ ὅσον ἀπόκειται τοῖς ἀνθρώποις ἅπαξ ἀποθανεῖν μετὰ δὲ τοῦτο κρίσις
verses.transliteration: kai kath hoson apokeitai tois anthrōpois hapax apothanein meta de touto krisis
verses.study_note: 'Appointed' (apokeitai — laid up, reserved, appointed as a destiny). 'Once to die' (hapax apothanein — to die once, a single death). 'After this the judgment' (meta touto krisis). Death is universal and unrepeatable — each person dies once. After death comes judgment — there is no second chance, no reincarnation, no annihilation. The parallel structure links human experience to Christ's experience: as men die once and face judgment, so Christ was offered once to bear sins and will return to bring salvation.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse establishes the finality of death and the certainty of judgment. Each person has one life, one death, and one judgment. This excludes reincarnation, soul sleep without judgment, and universalism. The parallel with v.28 shows that Christ's death was equally singular, definitive, and unrepeatable.

verses.kjv_text: So Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many; and unto them that look for him shall he appear the second time without sin unto salvation.
verses.original_text: οὕτως καὶ ὁ Χριστὸς ἅπαξ προσενεχθεὶς εἰς τὸ πολλῶν ἀνενεγκεῖν ἁμαρτίας ἐκ δευτέρου χωρὶς ἁμαρτίας ὀφθήσεται τοῖς αὐτὸν ἀπεκδεχομένοις εἰς σωτηρίαν
verses.transliteration: houtōs kai ho Christos hapax prosenechtheis eis to pollōn anenenkein hamartias ek deuterou chōris hamartias ophthēsetai tois auton apekdechomenois eis sōtērian
verses.study_note: 'Once offered' (hapax prosenechtheis — offered once for all). 'To bear the sins of many' (pollōn anenenkein hamartias — to carry up, to bear away the sins of many; echoing Isaiah 53:12, 'he bare the sin of many'). 'The second time' (ek deuterou — a second time). 'Without sin' (chōris hamartias — apart from sin, having nothing to do with sin; His second coming will not be to deal with sin — that was accomplished at the first coming). 'Unto salvation' (eis sōtērian — for the purpose of final, complete salvation). 'Them that look for him' (tois auton apekdechomenois — those who are eagerly awaiting Him). Three appearances of Christ: (1) He 'appeared' to put away sin (v.26, first coming); (2) He 'appears' in God's presence for us (v.24, present heavenly ministry); (3) He 'shall appear' a second time for salvation (v.28, second coming).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse presents the three-fold appearing of Christ that structures all of Christian theology: (1) His first coming — to deal with sin through sacrifice; (2) His present heavenly ministry — appearing before God on our behalf; (3) His second coming — to bring final, complete salvation. The second coming is 'without sin' — not to re-atone but to consummate the salvation already accomplished.

5.meaning: Mercy seat, place of propitiation, atonement cover — from hilaskomai (to propitiate, to make atonement). In v.5 the hilastērion is the golden cover on the ark of the covenant where blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement. It was the meeting point between God's holiness and human sin — where wrath was averted by substitutionary blood. Paul uses the same word in Romans 3:25 to describe Christ Himself as the 'propitiation' — He is the true mercy seat, the place where God's justice and mercy meet.

12.meaning: Redemption, ransom, deliverance effected by payment — from lytron (a ransom price). In v.12 Christ obtained 'eternal redemption' (aiōnian lytrōsin). Redemption implies captivity (to sin and death) and a price paid for release (Christ's blood). The redemption is 'eternal' — not temporary like the annual Day of Atonement cleansing but permanent, once-for-all, and everlasting in its effects.

14.meaning: Conscience, moral consciousness, inner awareness of guilt or innocence — from syn (with) + oida (to know). In vv.9,14 the conscience is the critical battleground: the old covenant could not purify the conscience (v.9); Christ's blood can (v.14). Conscience is the deep, inner sense of moral standing before God — the awareness of guilt that no external ritual can erase. Only the blood of Christ reaches this deep, cleansing not just the hands but the heart.

22.meaning: Shedding of blood, pouring out of blood — from haima (blood) + ekchysis (pouring out). A NT hapax (only in v.22). The compound word emphasizes the violent, sacrificial nature of blood-shedding: blood must be poured out — a life must be given — for sin to be forgiven. This is the foundational principle of biblical atonement, grounded in Leviticus 17:11.

26.meaning: Annulment, setting aside, cancellation, abolition — from atheteō (to set aside, to reject, to nullify). In v.26 Christ appeared 'for the annulment of sin' (eis athetēsin hamartias). Sin is not merely covered (as under the old covenant) but annulled — cancelled, abolished, set aside permanently. The same word was used in 7:18 for the 'disannulling' of the old commandment. What was done to the old law, Christ's sacrifice does to sin itself — it sets it aside once and for all.

28.meaning: To eagerly await, to wait with intense expectation — from apo (from, intensifying) + ekdechomai (to expect, to wait for). In v.28 believers are those who 'eagerly await' Christ's return. The compound prefix apo + ek intensifies the waiting: this is not passive patience but active, eager, straining-forward expectation. The hope of Christ's second coming characterizes genuine believers — they long for His appearing because it will bring the fullness of salvation.

meta.overview: Hebrews 10 concludes the doctrinal argument (vv.1-18) and pivots to application (vv.19-39). The chapter opens by reaffirming that the law's sacrifices were shadows that 'can never with those sacrifices which they offered year by year continually make the comers thereunto perfect' (v.1). Annual repetition proves inadequacy: 'in those sacrifices there is a remembrance again made of sins every year' (v.3), 'for it is not possible that the blood of bulls and of goats should take away sins' (v.4). Quoting Psalm 40:6-8, the author shows Christ's obedient self-offering replacing animal sacrifice: 'a body hast thou prepared me … Lo, I come to do thy will, O God' (vv.5-7). 'By the which will we are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all' (v.10). While Levitical priests stand daily offering repeatedly, Christ 'after he had offered one sacrifice for sins for ever, sat down on the right hand of God' (v.12). 'For by one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified' (v.14). The Holy Spirit witnesses through Jeremiah 31: 'their sins and iniquities will I remember no more. Now where remission of these is, there is no more offering for sin' (vv.17-18). The application begins: 'Having therefore, brethren, boldness to enter into the holiest by the blood of Jesus, by a new and living way … let us draw near … let us hold fast … let us consider one another' (vv.19-25). The fourth warning follows: 'if we sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins' (v.26). 'It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God' (v.31). The chapter ends with encouragement: remember your former endurance (vv.32-34), do not 'cast away your confidence' (v.35), for 'ye have need of patience' (v.36), and 'the just shall live by faith' (v.38).
meta.theme: The Perfected Sacrifice, the New and Living Way, and the Fourth Warning
meta.keyWordMeaning: Once / once for all — the decisive words of Hebrews' sacrificial theology. Hapax (once) and its intensified form ephapax (once for all) express the unrepeatable nature of Christ's sacrifice. In 10:10 'once for all' (ephapax) describes the offering of Christ's body; in 10:12 'one sacrifice for sins for ever' captures the permanent sufficiency. The word stands against all repetition, all supplementation, all re-offering. Christ's sacrifice is complete, final, and all-sufficient.
outline: The Shadow Cannot Perfect (vv.1-4): For the law having a shadow of good things to come and not the very image of the things can never with those sacrifices which they offered year by year continually make the comers thereunto perfect; for then would they not have ceased to be offered because that the worshippers once purged should have had no more conscience of sins; but in those sacrifices there is a remembrance again made of sins every year; for it is not possible that the blood of bulls and of goats should take away sins

Christ's Obedient Self-Offering (vv.5-10): Wherefore when he cometh into the world he saith Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not but a body hast thou prepared me; in burnt offerings and sacrifices for sin thou hast had no pleasure; then said I Lo I come in the volume of the book it is written of me to do thy will O God; above when he said Sacrifice and offering and burnt offerings and offering for sin thou wouldest not neither hadst pleasure therein which are offered by the law; then said he Lo I come to do thy will O God he taketh away the first that he may establish the second; by the which will we are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all

One Sacrifice: Sat Down Forever (vv.11-18): And every priest standeth daily ministering and offering oftentimes the same sacrifices which can never take away sins; but this man after he had offered one sacrifice for sins for ever sat down on the right hand of God; from henceforth expecting till his enemies be made his footstool; for by one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified; whereof the Holy Ghost also is a witness to us for after that he had said before This is the covenant that I will make with them after those days saith the Lord I will put my laws into their hearts and in their minds will I write them; and their sins and iniquities will I remember no more; now where remission of these is there is no more offering for sin

The New and Living Way: Three Exhortations (vv.19-25): Having therefore brethren boldness to enter into the holiest by the blood of Jesus by a new and living way which he hath consecrated for us through the veil that is to say his flesh; and having an high priest over the house of God; let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith having our hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience and our bodies washed with pure water; let us hold fast the profession of our faith without wavering for he is faithful that promised; and let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works; not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together as the manner of some is but exhorting one another and so much the more as ye see the day approaching

The Fourth Warning: Wilful Sin (vv.26-31): For if we sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins but a certain fearful looking for of judgment and fiery indignation which shall devour the adversaries; he that despised Moses law died without mercy under two or three witnesses; of how much sorer punishment suppose ye shall he be thought worthy who hath trodden under foot the Son of God and hath counted the blood of the covenant wherewith he was sanctified an unholy thing and hath done despite unto the Spirit of grace; for we know him that hath said Vengeance belongeth unto me I will recompense saith the Lord and again The Lord shall judge his people; it is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God

Call to Endurance and Faith (vv.32-39): But call to remembrance the former days in which after ye were illuminated ye endured a great fight of afflictions; partly whilst ye were made a gazingstock both by reproaches and afflictions and partly whilst ye became companions of them that were so used; for ye had compassion of me in my bonds and took joyfully the spoiling of your goods knowing in yourselves that ye have in heaven a better and an enduring substance; cast not away therefore your confidence which hath great recompence of reward; for ye have need of patience that after ye have done the will of God ye might receive the promise; for yet a little while and he that shall come will come and will not tarry; now the just shall live by faith but if any man draw back my soul shall have no pleasure in him; but we are not of them who draw back unto perdition but of them that believe to the saving of the soul

verses.kjv_text: For the law having a shadow of good things to come, and not the very image of the things, can never with those sacrifices which they offered year by year continually make the comers thereunto perfect.
verses.original_text: σκιὰν γὰρ ἔχων ὁ νόμος τῶν μελλόντων ἀγαθῶν οὐκ αὐτὴν τὴν εἰκόνα τῶν πραγμάτων κατ᾽ ἐνιαυτὸν ταῖς αὐταῖς θυσίαις ἃς προσφέρουσιν εἰς τὸ διηνεκὲς οὐδέποτε δύναται τοὺς προσερχομένους τελειῶσαι
verses.transliteration: skian gar echōn ho nomos tōn mellontōn agathōn ouk autēn tēn eikona tōn pragmatōn kat eniauton tais autais thysiais has prospherousin eis to diēnekes oudepote dynatai tous proserchomenous teleiōsai
verses.study_note: 'A shadow' (skian — a silhouette, not the substance). 'Not the very image' (ouk autēn tēn eikona — not the exact representation; eikōn means likeness, image — the substance itself). The law provides a shadow; Christ is the image — the reality. 'Year by year continually' (kat eniauton eis to diēnekes — annually, perpetually). 'Can never' (oudepote dynatai — is never able, not at any time). 'Make perfect' (teleiōsai — bring to completion). The annual repetition proves the law's sacrifices are permanently insufficient — they can never achieve what they repeatedly attempt.
verses.doctrinal_note: The shadow/image distinction is fundamental: the old covenant was the shadow; Christ is the reality. Shadows are valuable — they reveal shape and direction — but they have no substance. The law's sacrifices pointed to Christ but could not accomplish what only He could do.

verses.kjv_text: For then would they not have ceased to be offered? because that the worshippers once purged should have had no more conscience of sins.
verses.original_text: ἐπεὶ οὐκ ἂν ἐπαύσαντο προσφερόμεναι διὰ τὸ μηδεμίαν ἔχειν ἔτι συνείδησιν ἁμαρτιῶν τοὺς λατρεύοντας ἅπαξ κεκαθαρμένους
verses.transliteration: epei ouk an epausanto prospheromenai dia to mēdemian echein eti syneidēsin hamartiōn tous latreuontas hapax kekatharmenous
verses.study_note: The logic is irrefutable: if the sacrifices actually worked (removed sin permanently), they would have stopped being offered. The worshippers, 'once purged' (hapax kekatharmenous — once having been cleansed), would 'have no more conscience of sins' — no lingering guilt, no annual reminder. But the sacrifices continued year after year, proving they never fully solved the problem.

verses.kjv_text: But in those sacrifices there is a remembrance again made of sins every year.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ᾽ ἐν αὐταῖς ἀνάμνησις ἁμαρτιῶν κατ᾽ ἐνιαυτόν
verses.transliteration: all en autais anamnēsis hamartiōn kat eniauton
verses.study_note: 'Remembrance' (anamnēsis — a recalling, a bringing to mind again). Instead of removing the consciousness of sin, the annual Day of Atonement actually reinforced it — it reminded the worshippers every year that their sins had not been fully dealt with. The same word anamnēsis appears in Luke 22:19 ('this do in remembrance of me') — but the Lord's Supper remembers a completed sacrifice, while the Day of Atonement remembered an incomplete one.

verses.kjv_text: For it is not possible that the blood of bulls and of goats should take away sins.
verses.original_text: ἀδύνατον γὰρ αἷμα ταύρων καὶ τράγων ἀφαιρεῖν ἁμαρτίας
verses.transliteration: adynaton gar haima taurōn kai tragōn aphairein hamartias
verses.study_note: 'Not possible' (adynaton — impossible; the same word from 6:4,18). 'Take away' (aphairein — to remove, to carry off, to strip away). Animal blood is fundamentally incapable of removing human sin. An animal cannot substitute for a human being — it lacks moral agency, personal relationship with God, and voluntary consent. The entire sacrificial system was a divinely appointed illustration, not a solution. Only a human being could substitute for human beings — and only a sinless one could bear others' sins without being consumed by His own.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse states the absolute theological impossibility of animal atonement. Animal blood could never actually remove sin — it was always a temporary covering, a type, a pointer to the reality that only Christ's blood could achieve. The entire OT sacrificial system was preparatory and illustrative, never ultimate.

verses.kjv_text: Wherefore when he cometh into the world, he saith, Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not, but a body hast thou prepared me:
verses.original_text: διὸ εἰσερχόμενος εἰς τὸν κόσμον λέγει θυσίαν καὶ προσφορὰν οὐκ ἠθέλησας σῶμα δὲ κατηρτίσω μοι
verses.transliteration: dio eiserchomenos eis ton kosmon legei thysian kai prosphoran ouk ēthelēsas sōma de katērtisō moi
verses.study_note: Quoting Psalm 40:6-8 (LXX). 'When he cometh into the world' — the incarnation itself is a speech act. 'Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not' (ouk ēthelēsas — you did not desire/delight in). 'A body hast thou prepared me' (sōma katērtisō moi — you prepared/fashioned a body for me). The Hebrew text has 'mine ears hast thou opened' (dug/pierced); the LXX renders 'a body hast thou prepared' — the whole body for the whole person, made ready for obedient self-offering. God did not ultimately want animal sacrifices; He wanted the obedient self-giving of His Son — which required a human body.
verses.doctrinal_note: The preparation of a body for the Son is the incarnation: God fashioned a human body for Christ so He could offer that body as the perfect sacrifice (v.10). The shift from 'sacrifice and offering' (external, animal) to 'a body … prepared' (incarnate, personal) marks the transition from type to antitype, from shadow to substance.

verses.kjv_text: In burnt offerings and sacrifices for sin thou hast had no pleasure.
verses.original_text: ὁλοκαυτώματα καὶ περὶ ἁμαρτίας οὐκ εὐδόκησας
verses.transliteration: holokautōmata kai peri hamartias ouk eudokēsas
verses.study_note: 'Burnt offerings' (holokautōmata — whole burnt offerings, completely consumed by fire). 'Sacrifices for sin' (peri hamartias — concerning sin, sin offerings). 'No pleasure' (ouk eudokēsas — you did not take delight, you were not well-pleased). God ordained the sacrificial system but was never ultimately satisfied by it — it was always a temporary measure, always pointing to something better. The prophets repeatedly expressed God's dissatisfaction with mere ritual divorced from heart-obedience (1 Sam 15:22; Isa 1:11-17; Mic 6:6-8).

verses.kjv_text: Then said I, Lo, I come (in the volume of the book it is written of me,) to do thy will, O God.
verses.original_text: τότε εἶπον ἰδοὺ ἥκω ἐν κεφαλίδι βιβλίου γέγραπται περὶ ἐμοῦ τοῦ ποιῆσαι ὁ θεὸς τὸ θέλημά σου
verses.transliteration: tote eipon idou hēkō en kephalidi bibliou gegraptai peri emou tou poiēsai ho theos to thelēma sou
verses.study_note: 'Lo, I come' (idou hēkō — behold, I have come; present tense: I am here). 'In the volume of the book it is written of me' (en kephalidi bibliou gegraptai peri emou — in the scroll of the book it stands written concerning me). All of Scripture testifies to Christ (Luke 24:27; John 5:39). 'To do thy will, O God' (tou poiēsai to thelēma sou — to accomplish your will). This is the Son's response to the Father's dissatisfaction with animal sacrifices: 'I have come to do your will' — to offer the one sacrifice that perfectly satisfies God's justice and love.

verses.kjv_text: Above when he said, Sacrifice and offering and burnt offerings and offering for sin thou wouldest not, neither hadst pleasure therein; which are offered by the law;
verses.original_text: ἀνώτερον λέγων θυσίαν καὶ προσφορὰν καὶ ὁλοκαυτώματα καὶ περὶ ἁμαρτίας οὐκ ἠθέλησας οὐδὲ εὐδόκησας αἵτινες κατὰ τὸν νόμον προσφέρονται
verses.transliteration: anōteron legōn thysian kai prosphoran kai holokautōmata kai peri hamartias ouk ēthelēsas oude eudokēsas haitines kata ton nomon prospherontai
verses.study_note: The author draws out the interpretive significance of Psalm 40. Four categories of offering are listed: sacrifice (thysian), offering (prosphoran), burnt offerings (holokautōmata), sin offerings (peri hamartias) — comprehensively covering the entire Levitical sacrificial system. God 'did not desire' and 'had no pleasure' in any of them. 'Which are offered by the law' — these are not unauthorized offerings but the very sacrifices God's own law prescribed. Yet even God's own prescribed system did not ultimately please Him — because it was always intended to be temporary.

verses.kjv_text: Then said he, Lo, I come to do thy will, O God. He taketh away the first, that he may establish the second.
verses.original_text: τότε εἴρηκεν ἰδοὺ ἥκω τοῦ ποιῆσαι τὸ θέλημά σου ἀναιρεῖ τὸ πρῶτον ἵνα τὸ δεύτερον στήσῃ
verses.transliteration: tote eirēken idou hēkō tou poiēsai to thelēma sou anairei to prōton hina to deuteron stēsē
verses.study_note: 'He taketh away the first' (anairei to prōton — He removes, abolishes the first). 'That he may establish the second' (hina to deuteron stēsē — so that He may set up, confirm the second). The 'first' is the entire sacrificial system of the old covenant; the 'second' is Christ's obedient self-offering. The transition is not gradual but decisive: the first is removed so the second can stand. Christ's coming does not reform the old system but replaces it.

verses.kjv_text: By the which will we are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all.
verses.original_text: ἐν ᾧ θελήματι ἡγιασμένοι ἐσμὲν διὰ τῆς προσφορᾶς τοῦ σώματος τοῦ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ ἐφάπαξ
verses.transliteration: en hō thelēmati hēgiasmenoi esmen dia tēs prosphoras tou sōmatos tou Iēsou Christou ephapax
verses.study_note: 'By the which will' (en hō thelēmati — by this will, God's will that Christ came to do). 'We are sanctified' (hēgiasmenoi esmen — we have been and remain sanctified; perfect participle: a completed action with ongoing results). 'Through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ' (dia tēs prosphoras tou sōmatos — through the offering of the body). 'Once for all' (ephapax — once and never again). The sanctification of believers is accomplished by a single, unrepeatable act: the offering of Christ's body. This sanctification is not a process here but a definitive, completed reality — believers have been set apart by Christ's sacrifice.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse declares that believers are definitively sanctified — set apart for God — through Christ's once-for-all bodily sacrifice. The perfect tense 'we have been sanctified' (hēgiasmenoi esmen) emphasizes the completed, permanent nature of this consecration. While experiential sanctification is ongoing, positional sanctification was accomplished at the cross.

verses.kjv_text: And every priest standeth daily ministering and offering oftentimes the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins:
verses.original_text: καὶ πᾶς μὲν ἱερεὺς ἕστηκεν καθ᾽ ἡμέραν λειτουργῶν καὶ τὰς αὐτὰς πολλάκις προσφέρων θυσίας αἵτινες οὐδέποτε δύνανται περιελεῖν ἁμαρτίας
verses.transliteration: kai pas men hiereus hestēken kath hēmeran leitourgōn kai tas autas pollakis prospherōn thysias haitines oudepote dynantai perielein hamartias
verses.study_note: 'Every priest standeth' (hestēken — stands; the priest never sits because his work is never finished). 'Daily' (kath hēmeran). 'Oftentimes' (pollakis — frequently, repeatedly). 'The same sacrifices' (tas autas thysias — the identical sacrifices, over and over). 'Never' (oudepote — at no time ever). 'Take away' (perielein — to strip off, to remove completely). Standing, daily, repeatedly, same, never — five words that describe the futility of the old system. The standing priest is the perfect contrast to the seated Christ (v.12).

verses.kjv_text: But this man, after he had offered one sacrifice for sins for ever, sat down on the right hand of God;
verses.original_text: αὐτὸς δὲ μίαν ὑπὲρ ἁμαρτιῶν προσενέγκας θυσίαν εἰς τὸ διηνεκὲς ἐκάθισεν ἐν δεξιᾷ τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: autos de mian hyper hamartiōn prosenenkas thysian eis to diēnekes ekathisen en dexia tou theou
verses.study_note: 'One sacrifice' (mian thysian — a single offering). 'For ever' (eis to diēnekes — for perpetuity, permanently; modifies either the sacrifice or the sitting — both are true). 'Sat down' (ekathisen — He sat down). The contrast with v.11 is devastating: the Levitical priest stands (unfinished work) daily (constant repetition) offering the same sacrifices (ineffective); Christ offered one sacrifice (sufficient), sat down (finished work), forever (permanent effect). The seated Christ is the ultimate proof that atonement is complete.
verses.doctrinal_note: The session of Christ — sitting down at God's right hand — is the declaration that the work of atonement is finished. In the tabernacle there was no chair — the priest never sat because the work was never done. Christ sat down because there is nothing more to do. This single image demolishes every system that requires ongoing or repeated sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: From henceforth expecting till his enemies be made his footstool.
verses.original_text: τὸ λοιπὸν ἐκδεχόμενος ἕως τεθῶσιν οἱ ἐχθροὶ αὐτοῦ ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: to loipon ekdechomenos heōs tethōsin hoi echthroi autou hypopodion tōn podōn autou
verses.study_note: 'From henceforth expecting' (to loipon ekdechomenos — for the remaining time, waiting). Quoting Psalm 110:1 again. Christ's session is not passive; He is actively reigning, waiting for the final subjugation of all enemies. His priestly work is complete; His kingly rule continues until every enemy is under His feet. The 'enemies' include sin, death, Satan, and all opposition to God's reign.

verses.kjv_text: For by one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified.
verses.original_text: μιᾷ γὰρ προσφορᾷ τετελείωκεν εἰς τὸ διηνεκὲς τοὺς ἁγιαζομένους
verses.transliteration: mia gar prosphora teteleiōken eis to diēnekes tous hagiazomenous
verses.study_note: 'By one offering' (mia prosphora — by a single offering). 'He hath perfected for ever' (teteleiōken eis to diēnekes — He has completed/perfected permanently; perfect tense: the perfecting is accomplished and enduring). 'Them that are sanctified' (tous hagiazomenous — those who are being sanctified; present participle: their sanctification is ongoing in experience). The juxtaposition is deliberate: the perfecting is complete (perfect tense) while the sanctifying is ongoing (present tense). Christ's one offering has permanently secured the complete salvation of His people, even as they continue to grow in holiness.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the doctrinal summit of Hebrews' sacrificial theology. One offering — one! — has perfected forever those who are being sanctified. The perfect tense 'has perfected' (teteleiōken) means the work is done, finished, complete. The present participle 'being sanctified' (hagiazomenous) means the experiential outworking continues. Position: perfect. Practice: progressing. Both are secured by the single offering.

verses.kjv_text: Whereof the Holy Ghost also is a witness to us: for after that he had said before,
verses.original_text: μαρτυρεῖ δὲ ἡμῖν καὶ τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον μετὰ γὰρ τὸ εἰρηκέναι
verses.transliteration: martyrei de hēmin kai to pneuma to hagion meta gar to eirēkenai
verses.study_note: The Holy Spirit is invoked as witness (as in 3:7), confirming the argument from Jeremiah 31. The Spirit's testimony through Scripture validates the doctrinal conclusion: the new covenant achieves what the old could not.

verses.kjv_text: This is the covenant that I will make with them after those days, saith the Lord, I will put my laws into their hearts, and in their minds will I write them;
verses.original_text: αὕτη ἡ διαθήκη ἣν διαθήσομαι πρὸς αὐτοὺς μετὰ τὰς ἡμέρας ἐκείνας λέγει κύριος διδοὺς νόμους μου ἐπὶ καρδίας αὐτῶν καὶ ἐπὶ τῶν διανοιῶν αὐτῶν ἐπιγράψω αὐτούς
verses.transliteration: hautē hē diathēkē hēn diathēsomai pros autous meta tas hēmeras ekeinas legei kyrios didous nomous mou epi kardias autōn kai epi tōn dianoiōn autōn epigraphsō autous
verses.study_note: Jeremiah 31:33 quoted again (cf. 8:10). The internal writing of God's law — on hearts and minds — is the hallmark of the new covenant.

verses.kjv_text: And their sins and iniquities will I remember no more.
verses.original_text: καὶ τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν αὐτῶν καὶ τῶν ἀνομιῶν αὐτῶν οὐ μὴ μνησθῶ ἔτι
verses.transliteration: kai tōn hamartiōn autōn kai tōn anomiōn autōn ou mē mnēsthō eti
verses.study_note: 'I will remember no more' (ou mē mnēsthō eti — I will absolutely not remember any longer). This is the climactic promise: complete, final, permanent forgiveness. God's 'not remembering' is not forgetfulness but a deliberate, covenantal decision never to hold sins against His people again.

verses.kjv_text: Now where remission of these is, there is no more offering for sin.
verses.original_text: ὅπου δὲ ἄφεσις τούτων οὐκέτι προσφορὰ περὶ ἁμαρτίας
verses.transliteration: hopou de aphesis toutōn ouketi prosphora peri hamartias
verses.study_note: 'Where remission of these is' (hopou aphesis toutōn — where there is forgiveness of these sins). 'No more offering for sin' (ouketi prosphora peri hamartias — no longer any offering concerning sin). This is the logical conclusion of the entire doctrinal argument: if sins are fully forgiven (as Jeremiah 31 promises), then no further sacrifice is needed. The sacrificial system is not just superseded but rendered unnecessary. This single verse demolishes every system — whether Jewish or Christian — that requires ongoing sacrifice for sin. The work is done.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the formal conclusion of Hebrews' doctrinal argument (chs.1-10:18). If forgiveness is complete (Jer 31:34), no more offering is needed. The entire sacrificial system — Levitical and otherwise — is rendered obsolete by the completeness of Christ's work. From this point forward, the epistle moves to application: how should we then live?

verses.kjv_text: Having therefore, brethren, boldness to enter into the holiest by the blood of Jesus,
verses.original_text: ἔχοντες οὖν ἀδελφοί παρρησίαν εἰς τὴν εἴσοδον τῶν ἁγίων ἐν τῷ αἵματι Ἰησοῦ
verses.transliteration: echontes oun adelphoi parrēsian eis tēn eisodon tōn hagiōn en tō haimati Iēsou
verses.study_note: 'Having therefore' (echontes oun — since we have; drawing the practical conclusion from the doctrinal argument). 'Boldness' (parrēsian — confidence, freedom of speech, open access; the word from 3:6 and 4:16). 'To enter into the holiest' (eis tēn eisodon tōn hagiōn — entrance into the holy places/Holy of Holies). 'By the blood of Jesus' — the ground of our access. What was forbidden under the old covenant (only one priest, one day per year) is now the privilege of every believer: direct, bold, confident access to God's presence.

verses.kjv_text: By a new and living way, which he hath consecrated for us, through the veil, that is to say, his flesh;
verses.original_text: ἣν ἐνεκαίνισεν ἡμῖν ὁδὸν πρόσφατον καὶ ζῶσαν διὰ τοῦ καταπετάσματος τοῦτ᾽ ἔστιν τῆς σαρκὸς αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: hēn enekainisen hēmin hodon prosphaton kai zōsan dia tou katapetasmatos tout estin tēs sarkos autou
verses.study_note: 'New' (prosphaton — freshly killed, recently slaughtered; originally used of a freshly slain sacrifice, then 'new/fresh' in general). 'Living' (zōsan — alive). The way into God's presence is both new (prosphaton) and living (zōsan) — not an old, dead ritual but a fresh, living pathway. 'Through the veil, that is to say, his flesh' — Christ's body is the veil. When His flesh was torn on the cross, the veil of the temple was torn (Matt 27:51), opening the way into God's presence. His broken body is the door through which we enter.
verses.doctrinal_note: The identification of Christ's flesh with the temple veil is one of Hebrews' most striking images. The veil that once barred access now, torn open, is the very means of access. Christ's death — the tearing of His flesh — opened the way that His life, as the veil, had symbolically closed. The way to God goes through the cross.

verses.kjv_text: And having an high priest over the house of God;
verses.original_text: καὶ ἱερέα μέγαν ἐπὶ τὸν οἶκον τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai hierea megan epi ton oikon tou theou
verses.study_note: 'A great priest' (hierea megan — a priest who is great; echoing 4:14). 'Over the house of God' (epi ton oikon tou theou — over God's household; cf. 3:6). Two grounds for confidence: (1) the blood of Jesus opens the way (vv.19-20); (2) a great priest presides over God's house. Both are needed: access and advocacy, the way opened and the priest interceding.

verses.kjv_text: Let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith, having our hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience, and our bodies washed with pure water.
verses.original_text: προσερχώμεθα μετὰ ἀληθινῆς καρδίας ἐν πληροφορίᾳ πίστεως ἐρραντισμένοι τὰς καρδίας ἀπὸ συνειδήσεως πονηρᾶς καὶ λελουμένοι τὸ σῶμα ὕδατι καθαρῷ
verses.transliteration: proserchōmetha meta alēthinēs kardias en plērophoria pisteōs errantismenoi tas kardias apo syneidēseōs ponēras kai leloumenoi to sōma hydati katharō
verses.study_note: The first of three great exhortations ('let us'). 'Draw near' (proserchōmetha — let us approach; the word for approaching God, the priestly term for coming to the altar). 'True heart' (alēthinēs kardias — a genuine, sincere heart). 'Full assurance of faith' (plērophoria pisteōs — complete conviction of faith). 'Hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience' — alluding to the sprinkling of blood on the Day of Atonement; Christ's blood has cleansed the inner person. 'Bodies washed with pure water' — likely alluding to baptism, the outward sign of inward cleansing. Interior and exterior, heart and body, are both addressed.

verses.kjv_text: Let us hold fast the profession of our faith without wavering; (for he is faithful that promised;)
verses.original_text: κατέχωμεν τὴν ὁμολογίαν τῆς ἐλπίδος ἀκλινῆ πιστὸς γὰρ ὁ ἐπαγγειλάμενος
verses.transliteration: katechōmen tēn homologian tēs elpidos aklinē pistos gar ho epangeilamenos
verses.study_note: The second exhortation. 'Hold fast' (katechōmen — let us grip firmly, retain tenaciously). 'The profession of our faith/hope' (tēn homologian tēs elpidos — the confession of our hope). 'Without wavering' (aklinē — unbending, without leaning, without inclination to either side). 'For he is faithful that promised' (pistos ho epangeilamenos — the one who promised is faithful). The ground of perseverance is not our grip but God's faithfulness — we hold fast because He holds firm.

verses.kjv_text: And let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works:
verses.original_text: καὶ κατανοῶμεν ἀλλήλους εἰς παροξυσμὸν ἀγάπης καὶ καλῶν ἔργων
verses.transliteration: kai katanoōmen allēlous eis paroxysmon agapēs kai kalōn ergōn
verses.study_note: The third exhortation. 'Consider one another' (katanoōmen allēlous — observe one another carefully; the same intensive katanoeō from 3:1). 'Provoke' (paroxysmon — stimulation, incitement, sharp encouragement; the word can mean irritation [Acts 15:39] but here means positive prodding). 'Unto love and good works' — the goal is not irritation but inspiration. Believers are to study one another so as to spur one another toward love and action.

verses.kjv_text: Not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, as the manner of some is; but exhorting one another: and so much the more, as ye see the day approaching.
verses.original_text: μὴ ἐγκαταλείποντες τὴν ἐπισυναγωγὴν ἑαυτῶν καθὼς ἔθος τισίν ἀλλὰ παρακαλοῦντες καὶ τοσούτῳ μᾶλλον ὅσῳ βλέπετε ἐγγίζουσαν τὴν ἡμέραν
verses.transliteration: mē enkataleipontes tēn episynagōgēn heautōn kathōs ethos tisin alla parakalountes kai tosoutō mallon hosō blepete engizyousan tēn hēmeran
verses.study_note: 'Not forsaking' (mē enkataleipontes — not abandoning, not deserting). 'The assembling' (tēn episynagōgēn — the gathering together; from epi + synagōgē). 'As the manner of some is' (kathōs ethos tisin — as is the habit/custom of some). 'The day approaching' (engizyousan tēn hēmeran — the day drawing near, coming closer). Isolation is dangerous; community is protective. As 'the day' (the return of Christ, the final judgment) approaches, gathering becomes more urgent, not less.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse establishes the necessity of regular Christian assembly. The command is grounded in the approaching Day of the Lord — as the end draws near, believers need each other more, not less. Forsaking the assembly (which some were already doing) leaves believers vulnerable to the drift, hardening, and apostasy warned against throughout the epistle.

verses.kjv_text: For if we sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins,
verses.original_text: ἑκουσίως γὰρ ἁμαρτανόντων ἡμῶν μετὰ τὸ λαβεῖν τὴν ἐπίγνωσιν τῆς ἀληθείας οὐκέτι περὶ ἁμαρτιῶν ἀπολείπεται θυσία
verses.transliteration: hekousiōs gar hamartanontōn hēmōn meta to labein tēn epignōsin tēs alētheias ouketi peri hamartiōn apoleipetai thysia
verses.study_note: 'Wilfully' (hekousiōs — voluntarily, deliberately, intentionally; as opposed to sins of ignorance). 'After that we have received the knowledge of the truth' (meta to labein tēn epignōsin tēs alētheias — after receiving full knowledge; epignōsis is full, thorough knowledge, not casual acquaintance). 'No more sacrifice for sins' (ouketi peri hamartiōn apoleipetai thysia — no longer is there a sacrifice left for sins). This is the fourth warning passage. The 'wilful sin' in context is not any deliberate sin but apostasy — the deliberate, knowing rejection of Christ's sacrifice after having fully understood it. Since Christ's is the only sacrifice for sin (there is no Plan B), rejecting it leaves nothing.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is the fourth and most severe warning passage (cf. 6:4-6). The 'wilful sin' is apostasy: the deliberate rejection of Christ after full knowledge. The logic is: if Christ's sacrifice is the only sacrifice for sins (vv.1-18), then rejecting it leaves no other means of atonement. This is not describing a believer who struggles with sin but someone who knowingly, deliberately abandons the faith.

verses.kjv_text: But a certain fearful looking for of judgment and fiery indignation, which shall devour the adversaries.
verses.original_text: φοβερὰ δέ τις ἐκδοχὴ κρίσεως καὶ πυρὸς ζῆλος ἐσθίειν μέλλοντος τοὺς ὑπεναντίους
verses.transliteration: phobera de tis ekdochē kriseōs kai pyros zēlos esthiein mellontos tous hypenantious
verses.study_note: 'Fearful' (phobera — terrifying, dreadful). 'Looking for' (ekdochē — expectation, anticipation). 'Judgment' (kriseōs). 'Fiery indignation' (pyros zēlos — fire of zeal/jealousy; cf. Deut 4:24, 'the LORD thy God is a consuming fire'). 'Devour the adversaries' (esthiein mellontos tous hypenantious — about to consume those who are hostile). For those who reject Christ, only judgment remains — the unshielded fury of a holy God against sin.

verses.kjv_text: He that despised Moses' law died without mercy under two or three witnesses:
verses.original_text: ἀθετήσας τις νόμον Μωϋσέως χωρὶς οἰκτιρμῶν ἐπὶ δυσὶν ἢ τρισὶν μάρτυσιν ἀποθνῄσκει
verses.transliteration: athetēsas tis nomon Mōyseōs chōris oiktirmōn epi dysin ē trisin martysin apothnēskei
verses.study_note: The a fortiori argument again: rejecting Moses' law brought death 'without mercy' (chōris oiktirmōn — without compassion; the punishment was execution, Deut 17:2-6). If that was the penalty for rejecting the lesser covenant, how much worse for rejecting the greater?

verses.kjv_text: Of how much sorer punishment, suppose ye, shall he be thought worthy, who hath trodden under foot the Son of God, and hath counted the blood of the covenant, wherewith he was sanctified, an unholy thing, and hath done despite unto the Spirit of grace?
verses.original_text: πόσῳ δοκεῖτε χείρονος ἀξιωθήσεται τιμωρίας ὁ τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ θεοῦ καταπατήσας καὶ τὸ αἷμα τῆς διαθήκης κοινὸν ἡγησάμενος ἐν ᾧ ἡγιάσθη καὶ τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς χάριτος ἐνυβρίσας
verses.transliteration: posō dokeite cheironos axiōthēsetai timōrias ho ton hyion tou theou katapatēsas kai to haima tēs diathēkēs koinon hēgēsamenos en hō hēgiasthē kai to pneuma tēs charitos enybristas
verses.study_note: 'How much sorer punishment' (posō cheironos timōrias — how much worse punishment). Three descriptions of apostasy as three offenses against the three persons of the Trinity: (1) 'Trodden under foot the Son of God' (ton hyion tou theou katapatēsas — trampling the Son of God; treating Him as worthless, as dirt to be walked on). (2) 'Counted the blood of the covenant … an unholy thing' (to haima tēs diathēkēs koinon hēgēsamenos — regarding the covenant blood as common, profane, unclean; koinon means ordinary, not set apart). 'Wherewith he was sanctified' (en hō hēgiasthē — by which he was set apart; suggesting the apostate once participated in covenant blessings). (3) 'Done despite unto the Spirit of grace' (to pneuma tēs charitos enybristas — insulting, outraging the Spirit of grace; enybrizō means to insult with arrogance and contempt).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse describes apostasy as a threefold offense against the Trinity: trampling the Son, profaning the blood, and insulting the Spirit. The trinitarian structure mirrors the trinitarian benediction of 2 Cor 13:14 but in negative: the apostate rejects the grace of the Son, the love of the Father (expressed in the covenant), and the communion of the Spirit. No greater crime is conceivable.

verses.kjv_text: For we know him that hath said, Vengeance belongeth unto me, I will recompense, saith the Lord. And again, The Lord shall judge his people.
verses.original_text: οἴδαμεν γὰρ τὸν εἰπόντα ἐμοὶ ἐκδίκησις ἐγὼ ἀνταποδώσω λέγει κύριος καὶ πάλιν κύριος κρινεῖ τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: oidamen gar ton eiponta emoi ekdikēsis egō antapodōsō legei kyrios kai palin kyrios krinei ton laon autou
verses.study_note: Two OT quotations: (1) Deuteronomy 32:35 — 'Vengeance is mine, I will repay.' (2) Deuteronomy 32:36 — 'The LORD shall judge his people.' The sobering truth: God will judge not just pagan enemies but 'his people' — those within the covenant community. Judgment begins at the house of God (1 Pet 4:17). The God of mercy is also the God of justice.

verses.kjv_text: It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God.
verses.original_text: φοβερὸν τὸ ἐμπεσεῖν εἰς χεῖρας θεοῦ ζῶντος
verses.transliteration: phoberon to empesein eis cheiras theou zōntos
verses.study_note: 'Fearful' (phoberon — terrifying, dreadful; the same word as v.27). 'To fall into the hands' (empesein eis cheiras — to fall into the grasp of). 'The living God' (theou zōntos — not a dead idol but the living, active, responding God). For the unbelieving and apostate, God's 'living' nature is not comfort but terror — He is alive, active, aware, and will act. The same 'living God' who gives life to the faithful (3:12) is the terror of the unfaithful.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse, one of the most terrifying in Scripture, is the complement of 4:16 (the throne of grace). For those who come through Christ, God's presence is grace; for those who reject Christ, God's presence is judgment. The same God, the same throne, but the approach determines everything.

verses.kjv_text: But call to remembrance the former days, in which, after ye were illuminated, ye endured a great fight of afflictions;
verses.original_text: ἀναμιμνήσκεσθε δὲ τὰς πρότερον ἡμέρας ἐν αἷς φωτισθέντες πολλὴν ἄθλησιν ὑπεμείνατε παθημάτων
verses.transliteration: anamimnēskesthe de tas proteron hēmeras en hais phōtisthentes pollēn athlēsin hypemeinate pathēmatōn
verses.study_note: 'Call to remembrance' (anamimnēskesthe — recall, remember; the author shifts from warning to encouragement). 'After ye were illuminated' (phōtisthentes — after being enlightened; cf. 6:4). 'A great fight of afflictions' (pollēn athlēsin pathēmatōn — a great athletic contest of sufferings; athlēsis is the word from which 'athletics' derives). The readers had already endured severe persecution after their conversion — they are not untested. The author appeals to their history of faithfulness as motivation for continued perseverance.

verses.kjv_text: Partly, whilst ye were made a gazingstock both by reproaches and afflictions; and partly, whilst ye became companions of them that were so used.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο μὲν ὀνειδισμοῖς τε καὶ θλίψεσιν θεατριζόμενοι τοῦτο δὲ κοινωνοὶ τῶν οὕτως ἀναστρεφομένων γενηθέντες
verses.transliteration: touto men oneidismois te kai thlipsesin theatrizomenoi touto de koinōnoi tōn houtōs anastrephomenōn genēthentes
verses.study_note: 'Gazingstock' (theatrizomenoi — publicly exposed, made a spectacle; from theatron, theater). 'Reproaches' (oneidismois — insults, verbal abuse). 'Afflictions' (thlipsesin — pressures, tribulations). 'Companions' (koinōnoi — sharers, partners). They endured both direct persecution and solidarity with persecuted brothers — they did not abandon those who suffered but identified with them.

verses.kjv_text: For ye had compassion of me in my bonds, and took joyfully the spoiling of your goods, knowing in yourselves that ye have in heaven a better and an enduring substance.
verses.original_text: καὶ γὰρ τοῖς δεσμοῖς μου συνεπαθήσατε καὶ τὴν ἁρπαγὴν τῶν ὑπαρχόντων ὑμῶν μετὰ χαρᾶς προσεδέξασθε γινώσκοντες ἔχειν ἑαυτοὺς κρείττονα ὕπαρξιν ἐν οὐρανοῖς καὶ μένουσαν
verses.transliteration: kai gar tois desmois mou synepathēsate kai tēn harpagēn tōn hyparchontōn hymōn meta charas prosedexasthe ginōskontes echein heautous kreittona hyparxin en ouranois kai menousan
verses.study_note: 'Compassion of me in my bonds' (tois desmois mou synepathēsate — you sympathized with my imprisonment). 'Took joyfully the spoiling of your goods' (tēn harpagēn tōn hyparchontōn hymōn meta charas prosedexasthe — you accepted with joy the plundering of your possessions). 'Knowing … a better and enduring substance' (kreittona hyparxin en ouranois kai menousan — a better and permanent possession in heaven). They could endure the loss of earthly goods because they knew they had heavenly treasure that could never be taken. This is the faith that enables perseverance.

verses.kjv_text: Cast not away therefore your confidence, which hath great recompence of reward.
verses.original_text: μὴ ἀποβάλητε οὖν τὴν παρρησίαν ὑμῶν ἥτις ἔχει μεγάλην μισθαποδοσίαν
verses.transliteration: mē apobalēte oun tēn parrēsian hymōn hētis echei megalēn misthapodosian
verses.study_note: 'Cast not away' (mē apobalēte — do not throw away, do not discard). 'Your confidence' (tēn parrēsian hymōn — your boldness; the same parrēsia that characterizes faithful believers throughout Hebrews). 'Great recompence of reward' (megalēn misthapodosian — a great repayment, a magnificent return). The confidence they have demonstrated is not to be abandoned — it carries the promise of divine reward. Perseverance has a payoff; faithfulness will be rewarded.

verses.kjv_text: For ye have need of patience, that, after ye have done the will of God, ye might receive the promise.
verses.original_text: ὑπομονῆς γὰρ ἔχετε χρείαν ἵνα τὸ θέλημα τοῦ θεοῦ ποιήσαντες κομίσησθε τὴν ἐπαγγελίαν
verses.transliteration: hypomonēs gar echete chreian hina to thelēma tou theou poiēsantes komisēsthe tēn epangelian
verses.study_note: 'Patience' (hypomonēs — endurance, perseverance, steadfast endurance under pressure; from hypo + menō, to remain under). 'After ye have done the will of God' — the promise is received after endurance, not immediately. 'Receive the promise' (komisēsthe tēn epangelian — to carry off, to obtain the promise). The structure is: do God's will → endure → receive the promise. There is a gap between doing and receiving — and that gap is filled by patience.

verses.kjv_text: For yet a little while, and he that shall come will come, and will not tarry.
verses.original_text: ἔτι γὰρ μικρὸν ὅσον ὅσον ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἥξει καὶ οὐ χρονιεῖ
verses.transliteration: eti gar mikron hoson hoson ho erchomenos hēxei kai ou chroniei
verses.study_note: Quoting Isaiah 26:20 and Habakkuk 2:3 (LXX). 'A little while' (mikron hoson hoson — a very, very little while; the doubled hoson intensifies the brevity). 'He that shall come will come' (ho erchomenos hēxei — the Coming One will arrive). 'Will not tarry' (ou chroniei — will not delay). The wait for Christ's return may feel long, but in God's timeline it is 'a little while.' This promise sustains endurance: the delay is temporary; the coming is certain.

verses.kjv_text: Now the just shall live by faith: but if any man draw back, my soul shall have no pleasure in him.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ δίκαιος ἐκ πίστεως ζήσεται καὶ ἐὰν ὑποστείληται οὐκ εὐδοκεῖ ἡ ψυχή μου ἐν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: ho de dikaios ek pisteōs zēsetai kai ean hyposteilētai ouk eudokei hē psychē mou en autō
verses.study_note: 'The just shall live by faith' (ho dikaios ek pisteōs zēsetai — Habakkuk 2:4; the same text Paul quotes in Rom 1:17 and Gal 3:11). This is the bridge to chapter 11 — the great 'faith chapter.' 'If any man draw back' (ean hyposteilētai — if he shrinks back, if he retreats). 'My soul shall have no pleasure in him' (ouk eudokei hē psychē mou — my soul is not pleased). Two paths: faith that perseveres, or shrinking back that displeases God. The warning is against the latter; the encouragement is toward the former.
verses.doctrinal_note: Habakkuk 2:4 — 'the just shall live by faith' — is one of the most important OT texts in the NT. Paul uses it to establish justification by faith (Rom 1:17; Gal 3:11). Hebrews uses it to establish perseverance by faith. Both are correct: the same faith that justifies also sustains — faith is not a one-time act but an ongoing way of life.

verses.kjv_text: But we are not of them who draw back unto perdition; but of them that believe to the saving of the soul.
verses.original_text: ἡμεῖς δὲ οὐκ ἐσμὲν ὑποστολῆς εἰς ἀπώλειαν ἀλλὰ πίστεως εἰς περιποίησιν ψυχῆς
verses.transliteration: hēmeis de ouk esmen hypostolēs eis apōleian alla pisteōs eis peripoiēsin psychēs
verses.study_note: 'We are not of them who draw back' (ouk esmen hypostolēs — we are not of shrinking back; the genitive suggests character: we do not belong to the category of those who retreat). 'Unto perdition' (eis apōleian — unto destruction, ruin, eternal loss). 'But of them that believe' (alla pisteōs — but of faith). 'To the saving of the soul' (eis peripoiēsin psychēs — unto the preservation/possession of the soul). As in 6:9, the author expresses confidence in his readers: they are not headed for apostasy but for salvation. The verse is the perfect transition to chapter 11: from warning about faithlessness to the celebration of faith.

3.meaning: Remembrance, recollection, memorial — from ana (again) + mimnēskō (to remember). In v.3 the annual Day of Atonement served as an anamnēsis of sins — not a forgetting but a remembering.
